content.text:
```json
{
  "module": "scheduling",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 22,
    "domain_depth": 28,
    "data_richness": 18,
    "interaction_quality": 15,
    "ai_integration": 8,
    "mobile_first": 12,
    "performance": 25,
    "typescript_quality": 35,
    "error_handling": 20,
    "realtime": 18,
    "accessibility": 10,
    "security": 22,
    "differentiation": 12,
    "enterprise_readiness": 18
  },
  "overall_score": 19,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "scheduling-C5-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "interaction_quality",
      "title": "No interactive Gantt chart — schedule page lacks core visual component",
      "file": "src/pages/Schedule.tsx",
      "description": "The Schedule page has no Gantt chart rendering. Per PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA Section 6, the page MUST render phases as horizontal bars with color coding by status, critical path highlighting, dependency arrows, zoom levels, and drag-to-reschedule. Currently only a basic table or card list exists with no timeline visualization. This is the single biggest gap in the module.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The Schedule page renders activities as a simple list/table with no Gantt chart visualization, no horizontal timeline bars, no dependency arrows, and no critical path highlighting. AFTER: Add a full interactive Gantt chart section below the metric cards. Create a GanttChart component inline (or import from a new file). Use a horizontal scrollable container with a time axis header (showing weeks/months). For each schedule activity, render a horizontal bar positioned by start_date and sized by duration. Color bars by status: not_started (#9CA3AF gray), in_progress (#3B82F6 blue), completed (#4EC896 green), delayed (#E74C3C red). Highlight is_critical_path activities with a red left border (3px solid #E74C3C). Show percent_complete as a filled portion inside each bar. Add a today-line (vertical dashed orange line at current date). Each bar shows the activity name to its left. Bars should be 32px tall with 4px gap. The time axis should support week-level granularity. Use inline styles with theme tokens. Include a zoom control (Day/Week/Month buttons) that adjusts the pixels-per-day scale (4px/day for month, 12px/day for week, 40px/day for day view). Add baseline_start/baseline_end as ghost bars (gray, 50% opacity, positioned behind the main bar) when baseline dates differ from current dates. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "scheduling-C5-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "domain_depth",
      "title": "No schedule import from P6/MS Project files — cannot ingest real schedules",
      "file": "src/pages/Schedule.tsx",
      "description": "Per PRODUCTION_ROADMAP P0-2, schedule import from .xer, .xml, and .mpp files is a dealbreaker. Superintendents check P6 schedules daily. Without import capability, the schedule page shows only manually created activities. Add an Import Schedule button and file upload flow that parses XER/XML files client-side into the schedule_phases table format.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The Schedule page has no file import functionality. There is no way to upload a P6 .xer or .xml file or an MS Project .mpp file. Users can only manually create schedule activities. AFTER: Add an 'Import Schedule' button in the page header area next to any existing action buttons. Style it as a secondary button (white background, 1px gray border, icon of Upload/FileUp from lucide-react). When clicked, open a modal dialog with: (1) A drag-and-drop zone accepting .xer, .xml, and .mpp files with text 'Drop your P6 or MS Project file here' and a 'Browse Files' link. (2) File type auto-detection based on extension. (3) For .xml files, implement a basic parser that extracts activity elements: parse XML to find Activity nodes with ActivityId, ActivityName, PlannedStartDate, PlannedFinishDate, ActualStartDate, ActualFinishDate, DurationOriginal, FloatTotal, PercentComplete, Status. Map these to schedule_phases insert objects (name, start_date, end_date, baseline_start, baseline_end, percent_complete, float_days, status mapped from P6 status codes). (4) Show a preview table of parsed activities with columns: Name, Start, Finish, Duration, % Complete. (5) A 'Confirm Import' button that inserts all activities into schedule_phases via supabase. (6) For .xer files, parse as tab-delimited text: split by lines, identify TASK section by '%T TASK' header, extract columns by position. (7) Show loading state during parse and import. (8) Show success toast with count: 'Imported 47 activities'. For .mpp files, show a message 'MPP import requires server processing. Coming soon.' with the file queued. Add proper TypeScript types for the parsed activity interface. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "scheduling-C5-003",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "accessibility",
      "title": "Schedule page missing ARIA landmarks, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support",
      "file": "src/pages/Schedule.tsx",
      "description": "The schedule page has no ARIA attributes on any interactive elements. No role='table' or role='grid' on the activity list. No aria-labels on filter buttons, status indicators, or action buttons. No keyboard navigation for moving between activities. Per Global Acceptance Standards, all pages must pass axe-core with zero critical/serious violations and be keyboard navigable.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The Schedule page has no ARIA attributes. Interactive elements like filter tabs, sort buttons, and activity rows have no aria-labels. Status indicators use color alone with no text alternative. The page has no landmark roles. There is no keyboard navigation between schedule rows. AFTER: (1) Add role='main' and aria-label='Project Schedule' to the outermost page container div. (2) Add role='region' and aria-label='Schedule Metrics' to the metric cards container. (3) On each metric card, add aria-label that includes the label and value, e.g. aria-label='Total Activities: 47'. (4) On the activity list/table container, add role='table' and aria-label='Schedule Activities'. Add role='rowgroup' on thead/tbody equivalents. Add role='row' on each activity row and role='columnheader' on headers. (5) On each status badge/indicator, add aria-label with the status text (e.g. aria-label='Status: In Progress') so screen readers do not rely on color alone. (6) On filter/tab buttons, add aria-pressed or aria-selected as appropriate. (7) Add tabIndex={0} to each activity row and onKeyDown handler: Enter/Space opens detail, ArrowDown moves to next row, ArrowUp moves to previous row. (8) On any icon-only buttons (edit, delete, expand), add aria-label describing the action (e.g. aria-label='Edit activity Foundation Pour'). (9) Add an aria-live='polite' region that announces when filters change: 'Showing 12 of 47 activities'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "scheduling-C5-004",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "ai_integration",
      "title": "No AI schedule risk analysis or delay prediction on schedule page",
      "file": "src/pages/Schedule.tsx",
      "description": "Per PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA Section 6 criterion 7, the schedule MUST integrate weather forecast overlay and AI delay prediction. Per PRODUCTION_ROADMAP P2-2, predictive schedule delay alerts are a differentiator. Currently there is zero AI integration on the schedule page. No risk panel, no weather overlay, no delay probability. The ai-schedule-risk edge function spec exists in INTEGRATIONS.md but is not called.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The schedule page has no AI integration. There is no risk analysis panel, no weather overlay on the timeline, no delay prediction, and no call to any AI edge function. AFTER: (1) Add a collapsible 'Schedule Risk Analysis' panel below the metric cards and above the Gantt/activity list. Use a Card component with a header showing a sparkle icon (from lucide-react, Sparkles) and title 'AI Risk Analysis'. (2) Inside the panel, show 3 sections: (a) Overall Health indicator: a colored badge (green/amber/red) with text like 'On Track' or 'At Risk: 2 activities behind'. Calculate this from the data: count activities where status is 'delayed' or where percent_complete is < planned_percent_complete. If >20% of activities are behind, show red. If 10-20%, amber. Else green. (b) Critical Path Risks: list critical path activities (is_critical_path === true) that are delayed or have float_days < 3, showing activity name, days of float remaining, and a risk label. (c) Weather Impact: if any activities have outdoor_activity === true, show a warning card: 'X outdoor activities scheduled this week. Check weather before committing.' Style with amber background. (3) Add a 'Run AI Analysis' button that calls the ai-schedule-risk edge function via supabase.functions.invoke('ai-schedule-risk', { body: { project_id: projectId } }). Show a loading spinner while waiting. Display the response text in the panel. If the edge function is not deployed, catch the error gracefully and show 'AI analysis will be available when the AI service is configured.' (4) For the weather overlay concept, add weather icon indicators (Sun, Cloud, CloudRain from lucide-react) on the Gantt timeline header for each day if weather data is available from the weather_cache or weather_records table. Query weather_records for the project and date range, show icons on matching days. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "scheduling-C5-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "mobile_first",
      "title": "Schedule page not usable on mobile — no responsive layout or touch optimization",
      "file": "src/pages/Schedule.tsx",
      "description": "Per PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA Section 6 criterion 12, on mobile the Gantt should be replaced with a timeline list view where each phase is a card with start/end dates, progress bar, and status. Currently the page renders the same desktop layout at 375px width, causing horizontal overflow, tiny touch targets, and unusable interactions. Metric cards do not stack. No mobile-specific view switching.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The schedule page renders identically on mobile and desktop. At 375px width, content overflows horizontally, metric cards are too small to read, the activity table/Gantt is not scrollable or adapted, and touch targets are smaller than 44px. There is no responsive breakpoint handling. AFTER: (1) Add a useMediaQuery or window.innerWidth check (const isMobile = window.innerWidth < 768) using a useState + useEffect with resize listener. (2) For metric cards: on mobile, change the grid to gridTemplateColumns: '1fr 1fr' (2 columns instead of 4), with larger text (value font size 24px instead of 28px). (3) For the activity list: on mobile, replace the table layout with a vertical card list. Each activity card should be a full-width Card with padding 16px containing: activity name (16px, font-weight 600), a row with start and end dates (12px, muted color, formatted as 'Mar 15 — Apr 22'), a progress bar (height 8px, border-radius 4px, background #E5E7EB with fill colored by status), status badge (small Tag component), and float days shown as '3d float' text. Cards should have min-height 80px for easy touch targeting. (4) Add 8px gap between cards. (5) Hide the Gantt chart on mobile entirely (display: 'none' when isMobile). Show a small info text: 'Switch to desktop for Gantt chart view.' (6) Make filter tabs horizontally scrollable on mobile with overflow-x: 'auto' and white-space: 'nowrap'. (7) Ensure all tap targets are at least 44x44px. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [
    {
      "title": "3-Week Lookahead Generator",
      "description": "Auto-generate a 3-week lookahead schedule from the master schedule, filtered to only activities starting or in-progress within the next 21 days. Export as a single-page PDF formatted for field distribution at weekly coordination meetings. Include responsible party, crew size needed, and material delivery dates. This is what every superintendent prints and carries in their back pocket."
    },
    {
      "title": "Schedule vs. Weather Conflict Detector",
      "description": "Cross-reference outdoor activities (concrete pours, roofing, exterior paint, earthwork) against the 7-day weather forecast. If rain probability > 60% on a pour day, auto-generate a notification: 'Concrete pour on Phase 3 (Thursday) may be impacted by 70% rain probability. Consider rescheduling to Monday.' Link to the weather widget and the affected activity."
    }
  ],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore's scheduling is weak — they rely on third-party P6 integration and offer no native Gantt. Fieldwire has zero schedule capability. Buildertrend has a basic Gantt but no critical path analysis or P6 import. Autodesk Construction Cloud has strong BIM-based scheduling but poor mobile UX. SiteSync's opportunity is to be the first platform that combines P6 import, interactive Gantt with critical path, AI delay prediction, and weather-aware scheduling in a mobile-first interface. The 3-week lookahead generator alone would be a killer feature — every super needs it and currently builds it manually in Excel.",
  "summary": "The scheduling module scores 19/100 — the lowest-performing module in the platform. The fundamental issue is the absence of an interactive Gantt chart, which is the core visual metaphor for construction scheduling. Without it, the page is just a list of dates. There is no P6/MS Project import (P0 dealbreaker per roadmap), no AI risk analysis, no mobile optimization, and no accessibility support. The five prompts target: (1) building the Gantt chart with baseline comparison, critical path highlighting, and zoom controls; (2) adding P6 XER/XML file import with preview and bulk insert; (3) adding comprehensive ARIA landmarks, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support; (4) integrating AI schedule risk analysis with weather overlay; and (5) making the page fully responsive with a mobile timeline card view. These five changes would move the score from 19 to approximately 55-60, establishing a functional schedule page that a superintendent could actually use."
}
```
